---
title: "DADA2 for full length 16S nanopore"
author: "Kira Goff"
date: "2026-07-08"
output: html_document
---

This workflow is adapted for Oxford Nanopore full-length 16S amplicon sequencing.

Unlike Illumina workflows:
* Reads are single-end.
* The full-length (~1500 bp) 16S amplicon is sequenced directly.
* No paired-read merging is required.
* Variable read lengths are expected.

After completing this workflow, downstream community analyses may be performed using the script I made for other Illumina sequencing, or any or phyloseq/vegan/your favourite analysis workflow.

# R Basics

1) Changes to previous code chunks do not automatically propagate forward. If you modify a chunk, rerun that chunk and all following chunks.
2) Save your workspace if you wish to pause during processing.
3) The easiest way to keep your working directory correct is to save this Rmd inside your project folder.
4) Text following # is a comment and is ignored by R.

# Before running this script
Important notes:

1) This workflow is designed specifically for Oxford Nanopore full-length bacterial 16S sequencing from Plasmidsaurus.
2) Error patterns are unique to every sequencing run. Process each sequencing run independently.
3) This workflow assumes demultiplexed FASTQ files (one FASTQ per sample).
4) This workflow assumes primers have already been removed

# File naming requirements

Files should have simple names. Ideally these will reflect the downstream names.

Examples:
Sample01.fastq.gz/Sample02.fastq.gz/Sample03.fastq.gz
highsalinity.fastq.gz/tankbottom.fastq.gz

Avoid spaces and special characters.

**Do NOT use '.' in your file name before the file extension**

**Unique sample names are required.**

# SET UP

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

Completely clear the workspace.
```{r clear-workspace}
rm(list = ls(all = TRUE))
```


Install packages if necessary. Don't run this if you don't need to. 
```{#r install-packages, eval=FALSE}
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
    install.packages("BiocManager")

BiocManager::install("dada2")
install.packages(c(
    "ShortRead",
    "Biostrings",
    "ggplot2",
    "stringr",
    "here"
))

```

Load required packages.

```{r load-packages}
library(dada2)
library(ShortRead)
library(Biostrings)
library(ggplot2)
library(stringr)
library(here)
library(DECIPHER)
```

Use this to set the file extension of your files
Change to "fastq.gz", "fa.gz", "fasta", etc. as needed
```{r set-file-type}
file_ext <- "fastq"
```


# DATA IMPORT

```{r import-fastq}
# This builds an absolute path starting from your main project folder
path <- here("testdata", # a folder inside of the directory you launched the script from
             "raw" # a folder inside the first folder 
             )
# point this towards your raw data - if they're in folder with different names, just substitute those
# if the raw data is one folder deep, just pass one variable
fn <- sort(list.files(
    path,
    pattern = paste0("\\.", file_ext, "$"), 
    full.names = TRUE
))

print(fn) # this should list all of your files
```


Extract sample names.
If you put a '.' in your sample name, this will break your naming convention
```{r sample-names}
sample.names <- tools::file_path_sans_ext(
    tools::file_path_sans_ext(
        basename(fn)
    )
)
sample.names
```

# QC AND FILTERING

Remove reads containing ambiguous bases (N) so downstream doesn't break.
```{r remove-Ns}
fn.filtN <- file.path(
    path,
    "filtN",
    basename(fn)
)

# Filter out Ns, enforce long-read lengths, and sanitize quality scores
out <- filterAndTrim(
    fn,
    fn.filtN,
    maxN = 0,
    maxEE = 25,                    # Relaxed error threshold tailored for long R10 reads 
    minLen = 1000,                  # Drops short fragments/artifacts
    maxLen = 1600,                  # Drops extreme chimeras/amplicons
    rm.phix = FALSE,
    multithread = TRUE
)
print(out)
```

Nanopore can read 5' -> 3' or 3' -> 5'
Normally we'd fix directionality when removing primers
But this data already has its primers removed
So we're using a refernce sequence to set direction
```{r orient-reads}
path.filtN <- file.path(path, "filtN")
filtN_files <- sort(list.files(
  path.filtN, 
  pattern = paste0("\\.", file_ext, "$"), 
  full.names = TRUE))

path.oriented <- file.path(path, "oriented")

# Wipe the folder cleanly if it exists to avoid the write mode conflict
if(dir.exists(path.oriented)) {
    unlink(path.oriented, recursive = TRUE)
}

dir.create(path.oriented, recursive = TRUE)

# Define a standard 5' -> 3' (here we're using E coli)
# This ensures decipher knows exactly what "forward" looks like.
ref_16s <- DNAStringSet("")

for (i in seq_along(filtN_files)) {
    cat("Orienting file:", basename(filtN_files[i]), "\n")
    
    fq_raw <- readFastq(filtN_files[i])
    dna_seqs <- sread(fq_raw)
    
    # Combine the reference sequence and your data into one object
    # This places your reference sequence at index 1
    dna_combined <- c(ref_16s, dna_seqs)
    
    # Run OrientNucleotides telling it that index 1 is the reference
    dna_oriented_combined <- DECIPHER::OrientNucleotides(dna_combined, reference = 1)
    
    # Strip the reference sequence back out so only your reads remain
    dna_oriented <- dna_oriented_combined[-1]
    
    # Track which reads were flipped 
    is_reversed <- as.character(dna_seqs) != as.character(dna_oriented)
    
    # Flip the reversed reads to the same orientation
    qual_strings <- quality(fq_raw)@quality
    
    if(any(is_reversed)) {
        qual_strings[is_reversed] <- Biostrings::reverse(qual_strings[is_reversed])
    }
    
    fq_clean <- ShortReadQ(
        sread = dna_oriented,
        quality = SFastqQuality(qual_strings), 
        id = id(fq_raw)
    )
    
    writeFastq(fq_clean, file.path(path.oriented, basename(filtN_files[i])), mode = "w")
    
    rm(fq_raw, dna_seqs, dna_combined, dna_oriented_combined, dna_oriented, qual_strings, fq_clean)
    gc()
}
```



```{r plot-initial-quality}
# Look at the properly oriented files we just made
oriented_files <- sort(list.files(
    path.oriented, 
    pattern = paste0("\\.", file_ext, "$"), 
    full.names = TRUE
))

# Plot the first four
plotQualityProfile(oriented_files[1:min(4, length(oriented_files))])
```

Above, we used relatively loose filtering thresholds.
Here, we're narrowing in on the final one. 
You can use the next few blocks to help see the length distribution

```{r read-histogram}
lengths <- unlist(lapply(oriented_files, function(f) Biostrings::width(Biostrings::readQualityScaledDNAStringSet(f))))
hist(lengths, col = "skyblue", main = "Sequence Length Distribution", xlab = "Length (bp)", breaks = 30)
```
Check to see if your sample has a peak around 1320 as well - this happens in some species
This may or may not be real depending on your data


Overview statistics
```{r length-summary}
lengths <- unlist(lapply(oriented_files, function(f) Biostrings::width(Biostrings::readQualityScaledDNAStringSet(f))))
summary(lengths)
```


You can use this to check the upper and lower quadrants
```{r quadrant-check}
lengths <- unlist(lapply(oriented_files, function(f) Biostrings::width(Biostrings::readQualityScaledDNAStringSet(f))))
quantile(lengths, probs = c(0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2)) # adjust these as desired
quantile(lengths, probs = c(0.85,0.9, 0.95, 0.99)) # adjust these as desired
```


Set your final filtering parameters - this is relatively quick, so feel free to iterate
```{r final-filter-parameters}
path.filtered <- file.path(path, "filtered")
if(!dir.exists(path.filtered)) dir.create(path.filtered, recursive = TRUE)

# Regenerate sample names from files to ensure 1:1 index alignment
oriented_sample_names <- tools::file_path_sans_ext(basename(oriented_files))
filt <- file.path(path.filtered, paste0(oriented_sample_names, "_filt.fastq"))

track <- filterAndTrim(
    fwd = oriented_files, 
    filt = filt,
    maxN = 0,
    minLen = 1250,   
    maxLen = 1350,   
    maxEE = 25,      
    truncQ = 0,      
    compress = FALSE,
    multithread = TRUE
)

# Force it to be a matrix if it's a single sample
if (is.null(dim(track))) {
    track <- matrix(track, nrow = 1, dimnames = list(oriented_sample_names, names(track)))
}

print(track)
```

Look at the overview stats below 
Generally speaking,for a good sequencing run, we want to keep ~65-85% of the reads from raw reads to final filtered reads

```{r save-filter-output}
# Convert matrices to data frames and preserve sample names as a column
df_out <- as.data.frame(out)
colnames(df_out) <- c("raw_reads", "filtN_reads")
df_out$Sample <- tools::file_path_sans_ext(basename(rownames(df_out)))

df_track <- as.data.frame(track)
colnames(df_track) <- c("oriented_reads", "final_filtered_reads")
df_track$Sample <- tools::file_path_sans_ext(basename(rownames(df_track)))

# Merge safely by Sample name
track_table <- merge(df_out, df_track, by = "Sample", all = TRUE)

# Set the row names back for standard look and print
rownames(track_table) <- track_table$Sample
print(track_table)

write.csv(track_table, "final-filter.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```

```{r recheck-qplots}
plotQualityProfile(filt[1:min(4, length(filt))])
```

This doesn't do too much for nanopore data in terms of unique sequences
But it helps prep stats for downstream
```{r dereplicate}
# Dereplicate
derep <- derepFastq(
    filt,
    verbose = TRUE
)

# If it's a single sample, wrap it in a list so it can be renamed
if (inherits(derep, "derep")) {
  derep <- list(derep)
}

# Apply sample name
names(derep) <- sample.names
```

# OTU CLUSTERING

Because of the error rates and patterns in nanopore, DADA2 (even with the PacBio settings) struggles
Here, we are collapsing into OTUs, which will swallow some of the errors
Don't go below 95% similarity

```{r extract-and-pool-reads}
all_seqs <- unique(unlist(lapply(derep, function(x) getSequences(x))))
global_dna <- DNAStringSet(all_seqs)
```

Cluster reads at X% similarity
```{r cluster}
clusters <- Clusterize(
  global_dna, 
  method = "longest", # "overlap", "shortest", or "longest"
  cutoff = 0.05, # cluster at 95% similarity, change as needed (1-0.95=0.05)
  processors = NULL) # multi-threads

# Check the cluster count immediately before proceeding! 
# This needs to be a reasonable number unless you want to leave it running overnight
new_count <- length(unique(clusters$cluster))
cat("New number of clusters:", new_count, "\n")
```
Check the number of clusters and adjust your % similarity accordingly. 
You want a few hundred to a few thousand (ie, less than 2-3k)
But that is going to depend strongly on how similar the samples are
If you have disparate communities, the number of clusters goes up exponentially
Common thresholds: 99% (0.01), 97% (0.03), 95% (0.05)

Extract the representative sequences
```{r representative-sequences}
# grab the cluster column from the clusterize dataframe
representative_indices <- !duplicated(clusters$cluster)
clustered_dna <- global_dna[representative_indices]
```

Align final sequences
```{r align-sequences}
global_align <- AlignSeqs(clustered_dna, processors = NULL, verbose = TRUE)
```


# ASSIGN TAXONOMY

These next two steps take longer than the v4v5 because the region itself is much larger
```{r assign-taxonomy}
# Use 'clustered_dna' (no gaps) for database matching
taxonomy <- assignTaxonomy(
    clustered_dna, 
    "/mnt/work-drive/databases/silva/current/silva_nr99_vcurrent_toGenus_trainset.fa.gz", 
    multithread = TRUE
)
```

```{r assign-genus}
taxonomy <- addSpecies(taxonomy, 
                       "/mnt/work-drive/databases/silva/current/silva_vcurrent_assignSpecies.fa.gz"
                       )
```

# SAVE AND OUTPUT

Gather data, format for output, and see most abundant OTUs
```{r data-wrangling}
# Visual data check (removing sequence row names for display only)
taxa.print <- taxonomy
rownames(taxa.print) <- NULL
cat("Top taxonomic assignments:\n")
print(head(taxa.print))


# Build abundance tables from clustering
# Map raw sequences to their original sample abundances
# Create a master lookup vector of counts for every unique raw sequence across all samples
raw_seq_counts <- setNames(unlist(lapply(derep, function(x) x$uniques)), 
                           unlist(lapply(derep, function(x) names(x$uniques))))

# Extract cluster membership mapping
# Ensure we are handling whatever format Clusterize outputted (data.frame vs vector)
cluster_assignments <- if(is.data.frame(clusters)) clusters$cluster else clusters
names(cluster_assignments) <- all_seqs

# Sum up the abundances of all raw sequences within each cluster
# Map cluster IDs back to rows, making an OTU/ASV count matrix
cluster_names <- paste0("OTU", seq_along(clustered_dna)) # using OTU here since it's a 95% cluster
unique_clusters <- unique(cluster_assignments)

# Loop through samples to rebuild the sample x cluster abundance table
sample_names <- names(derep)
counts_matrix <- matrix(0, nrow = length(unique_clusters), ncol = length(sample_names),
                        dimnames = list(cluster_names, sample_names))

for (s in sample_names) {
    sample_uniques <- derep[[s]]$uniques
    seq_strs <- names(sample_uniques)
    
    # Use unname() here to strip out the name attributes to help out match()
    s_cluster_ids <- unname(cluster_assignments[seq_strs])
    matrix_row_indices <- match(s_cluster_ids, unique_clusters)
    
    # Generate and sort sample sums/otus
    if(length(matrix_row_indices) > 0) {
        sample_sums <- aggregate(unname(sample_uniques) ~ matrix_row_indices, FUN = sum)
        counts_matrix[sample_sums$matrix_row_indices, s] <- sample_sums$`unname(sample_uniques)`
    }
}
```

Final output tables

```{r save-data}
# Format Abundance/OTU Table
otu_table_transposed <- data.frame(ASV = rownames(counts_matrix), counts_matrix, check.names = FALSE)
write.csv(otu_table_transposed, file = "asv_table.csv", row.names = FALSE)

# Format Taxonomy Table
tax1 <- as.matrix(taxonomy)
rownames(tax1) <- cluster_names # standardizes IDs matching the count table

# Replace NA values with 'Unclassified'
tax1[is.na(tax1)] <- "Unclassified"

tax1_df <- data.frame(ASV = rownames(tax1), tax1, row.names = NULL)
write.csv(tax1_df, file = "taxonomy_only.csv", row.names = FALSE)
saveRDS(tax1, "taxtab.rds") # Saves an R object ready for phyloseq

# Merge and output final unified table
clean.taxa.table <- merge(tax1_df, otu_table_transposed, by = "ASV", all.x = TRUE)
write.csv(clean.taxa.table, file = "final_clean_taxa_table.csv", row.names = FALSE)

cat("All files successfully exported! You're done.\n")
```

Quick overview of our output!
```{r}
# Create a list where each element is a sample containing only its present OTUs
unique_otus_per_sample <- apply(counts_matrix, 2, function(x) names(x[x > 0]))

# Count how many OTUs have a score > 0 in each sample column
# if you have one sample, this will show you reads/OTU
sapply(unique_otus_per_sample, length)
```
These numbers are probably going to be lower than your v4v5, especially if you clustered at a 95% threshold.
This comes from a couple of sources:
- primer bias
- the ASV to OTU collapse
- the shallower sequencing depth
- the fact that archaea are mostly absent

```{r abundance-overview}
# Calculate relative abundance per sample (columns)
rel_abund <- apply(counts_matrix, 2, function(x) x / sum(x))

# Count how many OTUs are > 1% (0.01) in each sample
dominant_otu_counts <- apply(rel_abund, 2, function(x) sum(x > 0.01))

# Format into a clean summary table
summary_table <- data.frame(
  Sample = names(dominant_otu_counts),
  Total_OTUs = sapply(unique_otus_per_sample, length),
  OTUs_over_1percent = dominant_otu_counts,
  row.names = NULL
)

print(summary_table)
```
